options.text: Medicine and finance share common features in the way they are dealt with
The financial market might benefit from stricter regulation
‘Over-the-counter’ is a term used for illicit drug dealing
Drugs should be more controlled as they often show unwanted effects

items.solution_path: The OTC passage says OTC drugs can be bought off the shelf, while OTC derivatives carry 'a big risk of unpleasant side-effects' — and 'if they were drugs, they would probably be supplied only on prescription'. The analogy implies that derivatives should be more controlled. That matches B: stricter r

items.prompt: In what way does Mann shed new light on the role of Europe in the colonisation of the New World?

options.text: He points out important variations between the colonising countries.
He considers underlying similarities more relevant than superficial differences among colonist practices.
He specifically addresses the moral issues involved in colonisation.
He stresses the attraction of silver for all the European countries colonising South America.

items.solution_path: Mann's new angle on European colonisation: he REJECTS the standard contrast between English (cultivators) and Spanish (extractors) and argues for 'extraction by all European colonists'. That is underlying similarity overriding superficial difference — exactly what B says. Answer B.

items.prompt: How can the reviewer’s impression of Nicholas Thomas’s book best be summarized?

options.text: Its intricate structure will make it difficult for non-specialists to understand.
It is based on high-quality research and should be fascinating for the general reader.
Its overall message may be too depressing for the general public to take to heart.
It provides few new insights but is nonetheless well worth reading. PROVET ÄR SLUT. FINNS TID ÖVER, KONTROLLERA DINA SVA

items.solution_path: The reviewer calls Islanders 'a fine work of scholarship' AND 'a lucid and engrossing read', and hopes it makes 'its own fatal impact on the outmoded public understanding'. So: solid research + enjoyable for general readers. That matches B.

items.prompt: What are we told about British jingoism in the late 1800s?

options.text: It was deliberately encouraged by other European powers.
It was particularly widespread among workingclass people.
It was partly inspired by sentiments in Spain and France.
It was routinely used by the elite to keep citizens on their side.

items.solution_path: The 'Jingoism' paragraph closes with politicians worrying about a working-class electorate and concluding that 'an assertive foreign policy was the best way to appeal to the public' — both parties used jingoism to keep voters onside. That deliberate political use is exactly D.

items.prompt: What is implied early on in the text about the emergence of human language?

options.text: The Linguistic Society of Paris immediately realised its evolutionary beginnings.
Findings concerning its roots were rare during the 20th century.
Darwin’s theory was credited with having solved the problem once and for all.
Its early evolutionary history is today a concern mainly in religious circles.

items.solution_path: Early in 'On the Origin of Language', the Paris Linguistic Society banned discussion of language origins in 1866, and 'For more than a century afterwards, little was learned about the evolution of language' — i.e. findings on its roots stayed rare through the 1900s. That is B.

items.prompt: What is the main point here?

options.text: The recognition of new disorders is often slowed down by bureaucratic obstacles.
Attempts to spread the word about lesser-known disorders may be due to commercial interests.
Restless legs syndrome is much less widespread than some studies have claimed.
Production of medications is often delayed owing to lack of economic incentives.

items.solution_path: The 'Restless Legs Syndrome' paragraph traces how Glaxo Smith Kline mounted a press-release campaign and funds the supposedly 'nonprofit' RLS Foundation — the awareness-raising about this lesser-known disorder is being driven by commercial interests, exactly what B says.

items.prompt: What is the main point in this text?

options.text: Wagnerian sopranos are usually rather big.
Sopranos at the Salzburg festival need to be well-built to fit into their costumes.
Today’s famous soprano singers are mostly slender.
Very few present-day sopranos can match Joan Sutherland and Montserrat Caballé.

items.solution_path: The 'Sopranos' passage tracks a shift away from the large Wagnerian diva toward 'a new breed of female opera singers — all of them sylphs', with 'slim waists and scanty outfits'. That generalisation about today's famous sopranos lines up with C.

items.prompt: What is said about Otto Katz’s stay in Berlin?

options.text: It made it possible for him to become an international actor.
It was marked by amorous adventures.
It gave him the opportunity to attend classes in business management.
It was here he first encountered Communism.

items.solution_path: The Berlin section says Katz was 'almost as committed to glamour and sexual conquest as he was to Marxism-Leninism' and names Marlene Dietrich among his lovers. 'Amorous adventures' = sexual conquests. Answer: B.

items.prompt: What are we told in this text?

options.text: There are similarities between an individual’s problem solving abilities and writing style.
There are signs that bloggers are becoming increasingly addicted to dopamine medication.
Research has found that people who constantly talk are often also regular blog writers.
Researchers suggest that blogging in certain aspects can be compared to physical exercise.

items.solution_path: The 'Blogging' paragraph notes that 'blogging might trigger dopamine release, similar to stimulants like music, running and looking at art.' Running is physical exercise, so blogging is compared to physical exercise on the dopamine-release dimension — exactly what D says.

items.prompt: What is claimed concerning the NHS in relation to efficiency and treatment quality?

options.text: Larger health care units tend to give clear advantages.
Most patients are in favour of more centralised hospital treatment.
Neither efficiency nor quality is promoted at big hospitals.
Doctors and patients alike are now questioning decentralised care.

items.solution_path: The article argues that the NHS still wastes money through fragmentation — fax machines, attachment to small local hospitals, scattered surgical programmes — and that scaling up to larger units improves outcomes. That matches A: larger health care units tend to give clear advantages.

items.prompt: What is the writer’s basic impression of presentday research into the origin of language?

options.text: It provides a good example of the difficulties involved in studying complex mental phenomena.
It shows that significant progress has been made since the mid-19th century.
It can so far be characterised as unexpected as well as utterly disappointing.
It proves that even prominent linguists can go wrong when faced with the complexity of some languages.

items.solution_path: The closing paragraph reframes the disagreement: language origins remain hard 'just because they are humankind's most human traits. Nobody ever said that studying the fascinating but flawed human mind with that very same mind should be easy.' The writer's takeaway is that complex mental phenomena ar

items.prompt: What is claimed about Bolivian silver?

options.text: It helped to improve relations between Europe and China.
In the long run, exporting it to Spain cost far more than was financially acceptable.
In the end, working conditions at the mines brought production to a halt.
Its abundance gave rise to economic and social instability in opposite parts of the globe.

items.solution_path: The passage says Bolivian silver caused 'financial volatility and ultimately the bankruptcy of the Spanish state' AND 'fuelled unrest in China'. That is economic and social instability on opposite sides of the world — exactly D. Answer D.
